title: Reading compost moisture by touch
subtitle: The squeeze test and what different moisture levels feel like
excerpt: Correct moisture is the most common compost variable that can be fixed by hand in minutes. Too wet and the heap goes anaerobic and smells. Too dry and decomposition stops. The squeeze test gives an immediate reading with no equipment. Once you know what correct moisture feels like, you can correct the heap in one visit.
sourceNotes: HDRA composting technique notes. Garden Organic composting guide (2022). US Composting Council Compost Specifications (CCQS) moisture guidance. Rynk et al., On-Farm Composting Handbook (NRAES, 1992).

content.text: Optimal compost moisture is 50 to 60 percent by weight. At this level, a handful squeezed firmly releases one or two drops of liquid and holds its shape when you open your hand. The material feels cool and slightly sticky. This is the wrung-out sponge level: wet enough to support microbial activity, dry enough to allow airflow.
Put on a gardening glove. Reach into the heap to mid-depth, not the surface, which dries out faster than the interior.
Grab a handful of material and squeeze firmly in your fist for three seconds.
Open your hand and observe: how much liquid came out, and how does the material hold its shape?

Water streams out when squeezed: too wet. Above 70 percent moisture. Anaerobic and likely to smell. Fix: add dry carbon material (cardboard torn small, straw, dried leaves) and turn the heap to incorporate air. Remove the cover for a day or two.
One or two drops, material holds shape: correct. Between 50 and 60 percent. Leave as is.

No liquid released, material crumbles when hand opens: too dry. Below 40 percent. Decomposition has stalled. Fix: add 5 to 10 litres of water per cubic metre of heap, then turn to distribute the moisture. Cover after watering.
Material feels hot: temperature above 40°C. The heap is actively composting. This is positive. Check moisture is in the correct range and continue turning every 4 to 7 days to maintain activity.
Winter: heaps tend to get too wet without a cover. Check after heavy rain and add browns if needed.
Summer: heaps dry out faster. Check weekly during dry spells. Water and re-cover if the surface feels dry when you dig in 10 cm.
A heap with a high proportion of kitchen scraps (very wet inputs) can become too moist even without rain. Balance each bucket of kitchen waste with an equal volume of dry browns.
